relius_provision_text: Excise tax after 2 1/2 months (or 6 months). Any Excess Contributions (and income) which is distributed after 2 1/2 months, or 6 months with respect to a Plan Year in which the EACA requirements of Section 12.2 are met, after the end of the Plan Year are subject to a ten percent (10%) Employer excise tax imposed by Code §4979. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of this Plan, Excess Aggregate Contributions, plus any income and minus any loss allocable thereto, will be forfeited, if forfeitable, or if not forfeitable, distributed no later than 12 months after a Plan Year to Participants to whose accounts such Excess Aggregate Contributions were allocated for that Plan Year. Excess Aggregate Contributions are allocated to the Highly Compensated Employees with the largest Contribution Percentage Amounts taken into account in calculating the ACP test for the year in which the excess arose, beginning with the Highly Compensated Employee with the largest amount of such Contribution Percentage Amounts and continuing in descending order until all the Excess Aggregate Contributions have been allocated. If such Excess Aggregate Contributions are distributed more than 2½ months after the last day of the Plan Year in which such Excess Aggregate Contributions were made (six months in the case of Excess Aggregate Contributions under an EACA if all Employees who are eligible to participate are affected, as described in the Plan), a ten-percent excise tax will be imposed on the Employer maintaining the Plan with respect to those amounts. Excess Aggregate Contributions will be treated as annual additions under the Plan even if distributed. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the treatment of Excess Contributions/Excess Aggregate Contributions and the associated excise tax implications for the employer. They specify similar timelines for distribution and the conditions under which the excise tax applies, particularly in relation to EACA requirements.
variance_notes: The Relius provision focuses on 'Excess Contributions' while the Ascensus provision refers to 'Excess Aggregate Contributions.' Additionally, the Ascensus provision includes details about the allocation of Excess Aggregate Contributions to Highly Compensated Employees, which is not explicitly mentioned in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: (e) Treatment of excess amounts. Excess Aggregate Contributions attributable to amounts other than nondeductible voluntary Employee contributions, including forfeited "matching contributions," shall be treated as Employer contributions for purposes of Code §§404 and 415 even if distributed from the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Excess Aggregate Contributions will be adjusted for any income or loss up to the end of the Plan Year to which such contributions were allocated. The income or loss allocable to Excess Aggregate Contributions allocated to each Participant is the income or loss allocable to the Participant's Nondeductible Employee Contribution account, Matching Contribution account, Qualified Matching Contribution account (if any, and if all amounts therein are not used in the ADP test) and Elective Deferral account; for the year in which such contributions were allocated. Notwithstanding the preceding, the Plan Administrator may determine the income or loss allocable to Excess Aggregate Contributions in the manner described in Plan Section 7.02(B) (i.e., the usual method used by the Plan for allocating income or loss to Participants' Individual Accounts or any reasonable method), provided such method is used consistently for all Participants and for all corrective distributions under the Plan for the Plan Year. The Plan will not fail to use a reasonable method for computing the income or loss on Excess Aggregate Contributions merely because the income allocable is based on a date that is no more than seven days before the distribution. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the treatment of excess aggregate contributions, but they differ in their specific handling of income or loss allocation and the corrective measures for excess contributions. The Relius provision treats excess contributions as employer contributions for certain tax code purposes, while the Ascensus provision focuses on adjusting excess contributions for income or loss. Additionally, the related adoption agreement elections differ in structure and focus, indicating that they may not produce the same plan outcomes.
variance_notes: Relius emphasizes the treatment of excess contributions as employer contributions for tax purposes, while Ascensus details the adjustment of excess contributions for income or loss. The related adoption agreement elections also differ significantly, particularly in the context of eligibility and matching contributions.

relius_provision_text: (e) Application of ACP test. The Plan is required to satisfy the ACP test of Code §401(m)(2), using the current year testing method, if the Plan permits after-tax voluntary Employee contributions or if matching contributions that do not satisfy the ACP test safe harbor may be made to the Plan. In such event, only 'ADP test safe harbor contributions' or 'ACP test safe harbor contributions' that exceed the amount needed to satisfy the 'ADP test safe harbor' or the 'ACP test safe harbor' (if the Employer has elected to use the ACP test safe harbor) may be treated as Qualified Nonelective Contributions or Qualified Matching Contributions in applying the ACP test. In addition, in applying the ACP test, elective contributions may not be treated as 'matching contributions' under Code §401(m)(3). Furthermore, in applying the ACP test, the Employer may operationally elect to disregard what else with respect to all 'eligible Participants' (1) all 'matching contributions' if the Plan satisfies the 'ACP test safe harbor' and (2) 'matching contributions' that do not exceed four percent (4%) (3 1/2% if a QACA) of each Participant's 'Compensation' if the Plan satisfies the 'ADP test safe harbor' using 'matching contributions' (the 'basic matching contribution' or the 'enhanced matching contribution') and the 'ACP test safe harbor' is not satisfied. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: ACP Test Safe Harbor Matching Contributions
ascensus_provision_text:
ACP Test Safe Harbor Matching Contributions – In addition to the ADP Test Safe Harbor Contributions described in the Definition Section of the Plan, the Employer will make ACP Test Safe Harbor Matching Contributions, if any, indicated in the Adoption Agreement on behalf of each Eligible Employee for the Plan Year.

The Employer may make ACP Test Safe Harbor Matching Contributions to the Plan based on Compensation earned and Elective Deferrals made during a portion of the Matching Contribution Computation Period (e.g., on a payroll basis when the Matching Contribution Computation Period is Plan Year), and the Employer shall re-calculate ("true-up") the ACP Test Safe Harbor Matching Contributions to the Plan for the Plan Year. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions govern the ACP test safe harbor matching contributions, outlining the requirements for employer contributions based on employee elective deferrals. They also provide options for how contributions can be calculated and the eligibility criteria for participants.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes more detailed operational elections and specific conditions under which matching contributions may be disregarded, while the Ascensus provision focuses on the true-up process for contributions. However, both ultimately aim to satisfy the ACP test safe harbor requirements.

relius_provision_text: The Administrator may adopt a policy setting for reasonable, nondiscriminatory procedures and administrative rules relating to In-Plan Roth Rollover Contributions. Such a policy can prescribe administrative forms and limit the maximum number of such rollovers a Participant may make during a Plan Year. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means any contribution made either as a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover or an Indirect In-Plan Roth Rollover. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to In-Plan Roth Rollover Contributions, but the Relius provision includes specific administrative policies and limitations on the number of rollovers, while the Ascensus provision defines the types of rollovers without administrative details.
variance_notes: The Relius provision allows for the establishment of administrative rules and limits on rollovers, which is not addressed in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the related adoption agreement elections differ significantly, with Relius focusing on employer nonelective contributions and Ascensus on eligibility and allocation formulas.

relius_provision_text: "Designated Beneficiary" means the individual who is designated as the Beneficiary under the Plan and is the "designated Beneficiary" under Code §401(a)(9) and Regulation §1.401(a)(9)-4.
ascensus_provision_text: Means the individual who is designated by the Participant (or the Participant's surviving Spouse) as the Beneficiary of the Participant's interest under the Plan and who is the designated beneficiary under Code section 401(a)(9) and Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-4.
reasoning: Both provisions define 'Designated Beneficiary' in a similar manner, referencing the same legal standards under Code §401(a)(9) and Regulation §1.401(a)(9)-4. They both indicate that the beneficiary is designated by the Participant or their surviving Spouse.
variance_notes: The Ascensus provision explicitly mentions the Participant's surviving Spouse as a designator, while the Relius provision does not specify this. However, this does not significantly alter the meaning or function of the provisions.

relius_provision_text: "Distribution calendar year" means a calendar year for which a minimum distribution is required. For distributions beginning before the Participant's death, the first "distribution calendar year" is the calendar year immediately preceding the calendar year which contains the Participant's "required beginning date." For distributions beginning after the Participant's death, the first "distribution calendar year" is the calendar year in which distributions are required to begin under Section 6.8(b). The required minimum distribution for the Participant's first "distribution calendar year" will be made on or before the Participant's "required beginning date." The required minimum distribution for other "distribution calendar years," including the required minimum distribution for the "distribution calendar year" in which the Participant's "requiring beginning date" occurs, will be made on or before December 31 of that "distribution calendar year." [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means a calendar year for which a minimum distribution is required. For distributions beginning before the Participant's death, the first Distribution Calendar Year is the calendar year immediately preceding the calendar year that contains the Participant's Required Beginning Date. For distributions beginning after the Participant's death, the first Distribution Calendar Year is the calendar year in which distributions are required to begin pursuant to Plan Section 5.05(D). The required minimum distribution for the Participant's first Distribution Calendar Year will be made on or before the Participant's Required Beginning Date. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions define the 'Distribution Calendar Year' in a similar manner, governing the same plan feature related to required minimum distributions. The structure and language are nearly identical, indicating a high degree of semantic equivalence.
variance_notes: The primary difference lies in the reference to specific plan sections (Section 6.8(b) in Relius vs. Plan Section 5.05(D) in Ascensus), but this does not affect the overall meaning or outcome regarding minimum distributions.

relius_provision_text: "Life expectancy" means the life expectancy as computed by use of the Single Life Table in Regulation §1.401(a)(9)-9.
ascensus_provision_text: Means life expectancy as computed by using the Single Life Table in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-9, Q&A 1.
reasoning: Both provisions define 'life expectancy' using the same regulatory reference, indicating they govern the same plan feature related to life expectancy calculations.
variance_notes: The only difference is the phrasing; the Ascensus provision includes 'Q&A 1' which may refer to a specific question in the regulation, but this does not affect the fundamental meaning or application of the provision.

relius_provision_text: "Participant's account balance" means the Participant's account balance as of the last Valuation Date in the calendar year immediately preceding the "distribution calendar year" (valuation calendar year) increased by the amount of any contributions made and allocated or Forfeitures allocated to the account balance as of the dates in the valuation calendar year after the Valuation Date and decreased by distributions made in the valuation calendar year after the Valuation Date. For this purpose, the Administrator may exclude contributions that are allocated to the account balance as of dates in the valuation calendar year after the Valuation Date, but that are not actually made during the valuation calendar year. The account balance for the valuation calendar year includes any amounts rolled over or transferred to the Plan either in the valuation calendar year or in the "distribution calendar year" if distributed or transferred in the valuation calendar year. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to contributions to a retirement plan, but they define different aspects. The Relius provision focuses on the calculation of a participant's account balance for distribution purposes, while the Ascensus provision defines 'Annual Additions' which includes various types of contributions and forfeitures. The Relius provision is more specific to account balance calculations, whereas the Ascensus provision encompasses a broader range of contributions.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's focus on account balance adjustments for distributions and the Ascensus provision's broader definition of Annual Additions, which includes various types of contributions and forfeitures. The options/elections in the adoption agreements also differ in focus, with Relius emphasizing employer contributions and Ascensus including employee contributions and other specific types.

relius_provision_text: 'Required aggregation group' means: (1) each qualified plan of the Employer or any Affiliated Employer in which at least one Key Employee participates or participated at any time during the Plan Year containing the 'determination date' or any of the four preceding Plan Years (regardless of whether the plan has terminated), and (2) any other qualified plan of the Employer or any Affiliated Employer which enables a plan described in (1) to meet the requirements of Code §401(a)(4) or 410. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
In General – Except as provided in Plan Section 7.19(B), this Plan is a Top-Heavy Plan if any of the following conditions exist:

1. if the top-heavy ratio for this Plan exceeds 60 percent and this Plan is not part of any Required Aggregation Group or Permissive Aggregation Group of plans;
2. if this Plan is part of a Required Aggregation Group of plans but not part of a Permissive Aggregation Group and the top-heavy ratio for the group of plans exceeds 60 percent; or
3. if this Plan is a part of a Required Aggregation Group and part of a Permissive Aggregation Group of plans and the top-heavy ratio for the Permissive Aggregation Group of plans and the top-heavy ratio for the Permissive Aggregation Group of plans exceeds 60 percent. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to eligibility but focus on different aspects. The Relius provision defines a 'Required aggregation group' for determining eligibility based on participation of Key Employees, while the Ascensus provision discusses conditions for a Top-Heavy Plan, which is a different eligibility criterion. They do not govern the same plan feature directly.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is focused on aggregation for eligibility based on Key Employees, while the Ascensus provision is concerned with the Top-Heavy status of the plan, which is a separate consideration. The related adoption agreement elections also differ in focus, indicating different eligibility conditions.

relius_provision_text: Code §402(g) dollar limit. A Participant's Elective Deferrals made under this Plan and all other plans, contracts or arrangements of the Employer maintaining this Plan during any calendar year shall not exceed the dollar limitation imposed by Code §402(g), as in effect at the beginning of such calendar year, except to the extent permitted under Section 12.2(c) and Code §414(v), if applicable. The limit will be adjusted by the Secretary of the Treasury for cost-of-living increases under Code §402(g). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means Matching Contributions made pursuant to the Safe Harbor CODA formula described in Adoption Agreement Section Three, in an amount equal to (i) 100 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that do not exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation for the Plan Year, plus (ii) 50 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation but that do not exceed five-percent of the Employee's Compensation, if applicable. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions govern matching contributions related to employee elective deferrals, but they differ in structure and specific matching formulas. The Relius provision references a general limit under Code §402(g) and allows for adjustments based on cost-of-living increases, while the Ascensus provision specifies a tiered matching formula based on employee compensation percentages.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is more focused on compliance with IRS limits and does not specify a matching formula, whereas the Ascensus provision outlines a specific matching structure (100% up to 3% and 50% up to 5%). Additionally, Relius mentions safe harbor contributions, which are not explicitly detailed in the Ascensus provision.

relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding anything in this Section to the contrary, if, in the non-standardized Adoption Agreement, the Employer elected to apply the 410(b) ratio percentage fail-safe provisions, then the provisions of Section 4.3(m) shall apply. Furthermore, if the Plan includes Employer matching contributions subject to ACP testing, then Section 4.3(m) shall be applied separately to the Code §401(m) portion of the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: ACP TEST SAFE HARBOR MATCHING CONTRIBUTIONS
ascensus_provision_text: Means Matching Contributions described in Plan Section 3.01(F) or Plan Section 3.03(B). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to matching contributions under retirement plans, but they govern different aspects of the matching contributions. The Relius provision specifically addresses the Code §410(b) fail-safe and ACP testing, while the Ascensus provision focuses on ACP test safe harbor matching contributions without explicit mention of fail-safe provisions.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific references to fail-safe provisions and the application of Section 4.3(m), which is not present in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the Ascensus provision includes eligibility requirements that are not mentioned in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: "Contributions taken into account. For purposes of determining the ADP and the amount of Excess Contributions pursuant to Section 12.5, only Qualified Nonelective Contributions and Qualified Matching Contributions contributed to the Plan prior to the end of the twelve (12) month period immediately following the Plan Year to which the contributions relate shall be considered. A Participant's Elective Deferrals are only taken into account for purposes of determining the ADP for a Plan Year if allocated to the Participant's Elective Deferral Account as of a date within that Plan Year and are only made with respect to Compensation that would have been received by the Participant in the Plan Year (but for the salary deferral election) or that would have been paid within two and one-half months after the close of the Plan Year (but for the salary deferral election)." [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: If the Employer elects to take Qualified Matching Contributions into account as Elective Deferrals for purposes of the ADP test, then (subject to such other requirements as may be prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury) the Employer may elect, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner, to either include all Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test or to include only the amount of such Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address matching contributions and their treatment in the ADP test, but they differ in the specifics of how contributions are accounted for and the options available for employers. The Relius provision specifies the timing and conditions under which contributions are considered, while the Ascensus provision allows for more flexibility in how Qualified Matching Contributions can be included for the ADP test.
variance_notes: Relius has specific timing requirements for contributions and includes details about Elective Deferrals, while Ascensus focuses on the employer's discretion in including contributions for the ADP test without the same timing constraints.

relius_provision_text: (f) Ordering of tests. The determination of the amount of Excess Aggregate Contributions with respect to any Plan Year shall be made after first determining the Excess Contributions, if any, to be treated as nondeductible voluntary Employee contributions due to recharacterization for the Plan Year of any other qualified cash or deferred arrangement (as defined in Code §401(k)) maintained by the Employer that ends with or within the Plan Year or which are treated as after-tax voluntary Employee contributions due to recharacterization pursuant to Section 12.5. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
Means, with respect to any Plan Year, the excess of
a. the aggregate Contribution Percentage Amounts taken into account in computing the numerator of the Contribution Percentage actually made on behalf of Highly Compensated Employees for such Plan Year, over
b. the maximum Contribution Percentage Amounts permitted by the ACP test (determined by hypothetically reducing contributions made on behalf of Highly Compensated Employees in order of their Contribution Percentages, beginning with the highest of such percentages).

Such determination will be made after first determining Excess Elective Deferrals pursuant to the definition provided herein and then determining Excess Contributions pursuant to the definition provided herein. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address the determination of excess contributions related to Highly Compensated Employees, but they focus on different aspects of contribution testing. The Relius provision emphasizes Excess Aggregate Contributions and the treatment of Excess Contributions as nondeductible voluntary Employee contributions, while the Ascensus provision outlines the calculation of Excess Aggregate Contributions based on Contribution Percentage Amounts. The related adoption agreement elections also differ significantly in focus.
variance_notes: Relius discusses recharacterization of contributions and nondeductible contributions, while Ascensus focuses on the Contribution Percentage and the ACP test. The related elections in each template are not comparable, as they pertain to different eligibility and allocation criteria.

relius_provision_text: (f) Modification of top-heavy rules. The top-heavy requirements of Code §416 and the Plan shall not apply in any Plan Year in which the Plan consists solely of a cash or deferred arrangement which meets the requirements of Code §401(k)(12) and 'matching contributions' with respect to which the requirements of Code §401(m)(11) are met. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The provision in (1) above will not apply to any Participant to the extent the Participant is covered under any other plan or plans of the Employer and the Adopting Employer has provided in the Adoption Agreement that the minimum allocation or benefit requirement applicable to Top-Heavy Plans described in Plan Section 3.04(E)1. (or (b)(1)). Qualified Non-Elective Contributions may, however, be taken into account for such purposes. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to matching contributions and top-heavy rules, but they address different aspects of eligibility and conditions for applying top-heavy rules. The Relius provision specifically discusses the conditions under which top-heavy rules do not apply, while the Ascensus provision focuses on participant eligibility and the impact of other plans on top-heavy requirements.
variance_notes: The Relius provision is more focused on the conditions for top-heavy rules, while the Ascensus provision emphasizes participant eligibility and the interaction with other plans. Additionally, the specific options available in the adoption agreements differ, particularly regarding the QACA provisions.

relius_provision_text: An IRR and an IRT may be made only by a direct rollover. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Loans must be adequately secured and bear a reasonable interest rate. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions relate to distributions and rollovers, but they govern different aspects of plan features. The Relius provision specifically addresses direct rollovers, while the Ascensus provision discusses loans and the acceptance of rollover contributions. Although they both involve rollover contributions, they do not cover the same requirements or outcomes.
variance_notes: The Relius provision focuses on direct rollovers and required minimum distributions, while the Ascensus provision includes loans and the acceptance of rollover contributions from IRAs, indicating a broader scope of options.

relius_provision_text: The Administrator may permit an IRR or IRT either by converting to cash any non-cash investments prior to rolling over the Participant's distribution election amount to the In-Plan Roth Rollover Account or In-Plan Roth Transfer Account or, by rolling over the Participant's current investments to the respective Accounts. A Plan loan so transferred (if such transfer is permitted) without changing the repayment schedule is not treated as a new loan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: The value of the Roth IRA for purposes of this Plan Section 3.15 is the prior December 31 balance adjusted to include the amount of any outstanding rollovers, transfers, and recharacterizations under Q&As 7 and 8 of Treasury Regulation section 1.408-8. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: While both provisions relate to the handling of rollovers and distributions within retirement plans, they govern different aspects. The Relius provision focuses on the conversion of non-cash investments and the treatment of loans during rollovers, while the Ascensus provision addresses the valuation of Roth IRAs and the acceptance of rollover contributions. The options available in the adoption agreements also differ significantly in their focus and implications.
variance_notes: Key differences include Relius's emphasis on the conversion of investments and loan treatment, whereas Ascensus focuses on the valuation of Roth IRAs and the acceptance of rollover contributions from IRAs. The specific elections available in each adoption agreement do not align directly.

relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding any other Plan provision, an IRR or an IRT is not a rollover contribution for purposes of the Plan. Accordingly, the Plan will take into account the amounts attributable to an "in-Plan Roth rollover contribution" in determining whether a Participant's Vested Account balance exceeds $5,000 for purposes of Code §411(a)(1). In addition, an "in-Plan Roth rollover contribution" is not a distribution for purposes of Code §411(a)(11) (relating to spousal consent) and 3405(c) (relating to automatic tax withholding). Furthermore, it is not a distribution for purposes of applying any limitations that a Plan may impose with respect to the number of in-service distributions permitted by the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means any contribution made either as a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover or an Indirect In-Plan Roth Rollover. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address in-plan Roth rollover contributions, but the Relius provision specifically outlines the treatment of these contributions regarding rollovers and distributions, while the Ascensus provision defines what constitutes an in-plan Roth rollover without detailing its implications on distributions or other plan features.
variance_notes: The Relius provision includes specific legal references and implications for spousal consent and tax withholding, which are not addressed in the Ascensus provision. Additionally, the Ascensus provision lacks detailed adoption agreement elections related to employer nonelective contributions, which are present in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: A Participant must be eligible for a distribution in order to roll over a distribution to an In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution Account in accordance with this Section. A Participant may not make an IRR with regard to an amount which is not an "eligible rollover distribution" as defined in Section 6.15. If the Adoption Agreement, a Participant may take an in-service distribution only for purposes of electing an IRR to an In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution Account. If elected in the Adoption Agreement, a portion of the amount that is eligible to be rolled over to an In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution Account may be distributed solely for the purpose of federal or state income tax withholding for the Participant's anticipated tax obligations regarding the amount includible in the Participant's gross income by reason of the In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution (and the amount withheld for income taxes). The Administrator may limit the amount of the 100% withholding distribution to the amount the Administrator reasonably determines is sufficient to satisfy the Participant's federal and/or state income tax liability relating to the Plan distribution. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Distributable Events – Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, a Recipient may complete a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover at any time. Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, a Recipient does not have to be fully Vested in an eligible account to complete a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover. If a Recipient completes a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover of an amount that is eligible for distribution under the Code and/or Treasury Regulations, the Plan Administrator, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner as allowed under the Code and rules promulgated by the IRS, may establish operational procedures allowing a Recipient to elect to gross-up their distribution for voluntary tax withholding purposes. Except for amounts withheld pursuant to a voluntary withholding election by the Recipient, Direct In-Plan Roth Rollovers will not be considered distributions from the Plan for the purposes of Code sections 72(p), 401(a)(11), 411(d)(6)(B)(ii) or other Code sections pursuant to rules promulgated by the IRS. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address eligibility for in-plan Roth rollovers, but they differ in specific eligibility criteria and conditions for distributions. The Relius provision emphasizes the need for a distribution to be an 'eligible rollover distribution' and includes specific language about tax withholding, while the Ascensus provision allows for direct rollovers without requiring full vesting and does not specify the same conditions for distributions.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's requirement for an eligible rollover distribution and the Administrator's discretion on withholding amounts, whereas the Ascensus provision allows rollovers at any time without vesting requirements and focuses on operational procedures for tax withholding.

relius_provision_text: In determining Years of Service or Periods of Service for purposes of vesting under the Plan, Years of Service or Periods of Service shall be excluded as elected in the Adoption Agreement. For this purpose, a predecessor plan is described in Regulation §1.411(a)-5(b)(3). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means nonforfeitable, that is, an unconditional and legally enforceable claim against the Plan that is obtained by a Participant or the Participant's Beneficiary to that part of an immediate or deferred benefit under the Plan that arises from a Participant's Years of Vesting Service. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address vesting, but they focus on different aspects and have different structures. The Relius provision specifically discusses the exclusion of service years for vesting, while the Ascensus provision defines what it means to be vested and outlines vesting schedules for different contribution types. The options available in the Adoption Agreements also differ in focus and implications.
variance_notes: Relius emphasizes the exclusion of service years for vesting, while Ascensus provides details on vesting schedules for specific contribution types. The Relius provision includes specific elections for 100% vesting, which are not directly mirrored in the Ascensus provision.

relius_provision_text:
(g) Alternative forms of distribution. Death benefits may be paid to a Participant's Beneficiary in one of the following optional forms of benefits subject to the rules specified in Section 6.8 and the elections made in the Adoption Agreement. Such optional forms of distributions may be elected by the Participant in the event there is an election to waive the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity, and for any death benefits in excess of the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity. However, if no optional form of distribution was elected by the Participant prior to death, then the Participant's Beneficiary may elect the form of distribution.

(1) One lump-sum payment in cash or in property that is allocated to the Accounts of the Participant at the time of the distribution.

(2) Partial withdrawals.

(3) Payment in monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, or annual cash installments over a period to be determined by the Participant or the Participant's Beneficiary. In order to provide such installment payments, the Administrator may (A) segregate the aggregate amount thereof in a separate, federally insured savings account, certificate of deposit in a bank or savings and loan association, money market certificate or other liquid short-term security or (B) purchase a nontransferable annuity Contract for a term certain (with no life contingencies) providing for such payment. After periodic installments commence, the Beneficiary shall have the right to reduce the period over which such periodic installments shall be made, and the cash amount of such periodic installments shall be adjusted accordingly.

(4) In the form of an annuity over the life expectancy of the Beneficiary.

(5) If death benefits in excess of the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity are to be paid to the surviving Spouse, such benefits may be paid pursuant to (1), (2) or (3) above, or used to purchase an annuity so as to increase the payments made pursuant to the Pre-Retirement Survivor Annuity. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: FORM OF DISTRIBUTION TO A PARTICIPANT
ascensus_provision_text: Unless otherwise specified in the Adoption Agreement, if the value of the Vested portion of a Participant's Individual Account exceeds $1,000 and the Participant has properly waived the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity (if applicable), as described in Plan Section 5.10, the Participant may request (either in writing or in any other form permitted under rules promulgated by the IRS and DOL) that the Vested portion of their Individual Account be paid to them in one or more of the following forms of payment: 1) in a lump sum, 2) in a non-recurring partial payment, 3) in installment payments (a series of regularly scheduled recurring partial payments), or 4) applied to the purchase of an annuity contract. Notwithstanding the preceding, Qualifying Longevity Annuity Contracts may be distributed in any manner allowed under the Code or Treasury Regulations. In addition, non-recurring partial payments may be made from the Plan either before Termination of Employment or to satisfy the requirements of Code section 401(a)(9). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions govern the distribution of benefits to participants or beneficiaries, but they differ in specific eligibility criteria and the types of distributions allowed. The Relius provision includes specific references to pre-retirement survivor annuities and the ability for beneficiaries to elect forms of distribution, while the Ascensus provision focuses on the vested portion of the account and includes a broader range of distribution options.
variance_notes: Key differences include the Relius provision's emphasis on death benefits and survivor annuities, while the Ascensus provision is more focused on participant-initiated distributions and includes specific language about Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuities. Additionally, the Ascensus provision allows for non-recurring partial payments before termination of employment, which is not explicitly mentioned in the Relius provision.

relius_provision_text: Excess Deferrals. If a Participant has Excess Deferrals for a taxable year, the Participant may, not later than March 1st following the close of such taxable year, notify the Administrator in writing of such excess and request that the Participant's Elective Deferrals under this Plan be reduced by an amount specified by the Participant. In such event, the Administrator shall direct the distribution of such excess Deferrals and income to the Participant. The amount distributed shall be treated as a distribution of Excess Deferrals and income. The following conditions apply: (1) the Participant shall designate the distribution as Excess Deferrals; (2) the distribution must be made after the date on which the Plan received the Excess Deferrals; and (3) the Plan must designate the distribution as a distribution of Excess Deferrals. Regardless of the preceding, if a Participant has Excess Deferrals solely from Elective Deferrals made under this Plan or any other plan maintained by the Employer, a Participant shall be deemed to have notified the Administrator of such excess amount and the Administrator shall direct the distribution of such Excess Deferrals in a manner consistent with the provisions of this Subsection. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means those Elective Deferrals that either 1) are made during the Participant's taxable year and exceed the dollar limitation under Code section 402(g) (increased, if applicable, by the dollar limitation on Catch-up Contributions defined in Code section 414(v)) for such year; or 2) are made during a calendar year and exceed the dollar limitation under Code section 402(g) (increased, if applicable, by the dollar limitation on Catch-up Contributions defined in Code section 414(v)) for the Participant's taxable year but counting only Elective Deferrals made under this Plan and any other plan, contract, or arrangement maintained by the Employer. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: "Notwithstanding the preceding, Qualified Nonelective Contributions cannot be taken into account in determining the ADP for a Plan Year for an NHCE to the extent such contributions exceed the product of that NHCE's 414(s) Compensation and the greater of five percent (5%) or two (2) times the Plan's 'representative contribution rate'. Any Qualified Nonelective Contributions taken into account under an ACP test under Regulation §1.401(m)-2(a)(5)(ii) (including the determination of the 'representative contribution rate' for purposes of Regulation §1.401(m)-2(a)(6)(v)(B)) is not permitted to be taken into account for purposes of this Subsection. Notwithstanding the above, Qualified Nonelective Contributions that are made in connection with an Employer's obligation to pay prevailing wages under the Davis-Bacon Act (46 Stat. 1494), Public Law 71-798, Service Contract Act of 1965 (79 Stat. 1965), Public Law 89-286, or similar legislation can be taken into account for a Plan Year for an NHCE to the extent such contributions do not exceed 10 percent (10%) of that NHCE's 414(s) Compensation." relius_provision_text: (g) Corrective contributions. relius_provision_text: A Participant may withdraw amounts from the Participant's In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution Account or In-Plan Roth Transfer Account only when the Participant is eligible for a distribution from the Plan account that is the source of the IRR or IRT. This Section does not expand (except, if elected, for distributions for withholding) or eliminate any distribution rights on amounts that a Participant elects to treat as an IRR or IRT. ascensus_provision_text: Distributable Events – Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, a Recipient may complete a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover at any time. If a Recipient completes a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover of an amount that is eligible for distribution under the Code and/or Treasury Regulations, the Plan Administrator, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner as allowed under the Code and rules promulgated by the IRS, may establish operational procedures allowing a Recipient to elect to gross-up their distribution for voluntary tax withholding purposes. Except for amounts withheld pursuant to a voluntary withholding election by the Recipient, Direct In-Plan Roth Rollovers will not be considered distributions from the Plan for the purposes of Code sections 72(p), 401(a)(11), 411(d)(6)(B)(ii) or other Code sections pursuant to rules promulgated by the IRS. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (g)(1) A Qualified Nonselective Contribution, if specified on the Adoption Agreement, shall be allocated to those NonHighly Compensated Employees specified on the Adoption Agreement in an amount sufficient to satisfy the ACP Test. As indicated on the Adoption Agreement, such contribution shall be allocated in (1) the same dollar amount, or (2) in the proportion that each NonHighly Compensated Employee's 414(s) Compensation for the year bears to the total 414(s) Compensation of all NonHighly Compensated Employees for such year, or (3) using the "bottom-up" method whereby such contribution shall be allocated to the Qualified Nonselective Contribution Account of the NonHighly Compensated Participant having the lowest 414(s) Compensation, until the applicable ACP test set forth in Section 12.7 is satisfied, or until such NonHighly Compensated Participant has received the lesser of the maximum "annual addition" pursuant to Section 4.4 or the maximum that may be taken into account in the ACP test pursuant to Section 12.4(g) (Targeted Contributions), continuing this process until one of the tests set forth in Section 12.7 is satisfied. ascensus_provision_text: Means Matching Contributions made pursuant to the Safe Harbor CODA formula described in Adoption Agreement Section Three, in an amount equal to (i) 100 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that do not exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation for the Plan Year, plus (ii) 50 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation but that do not exceed five-percent of the Employee's Compensation, if applicable. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (g)(2) A Qualified Matching Contribution, if specified on the Adoption Agreement, shall be allocated to those NonHighly Compensated Employees specified on the Adoption Agreement in an amount sufficient to satisfy the ACP Test. As indicated on the Adoption Agreement, such contribution shall be allocated in (1) the same dollar amount, or (2) in the proportion that each NonHighly Compensated Employee's Elective Deferrals for the year bears to the total Elective Deferrals of all NonHighly Compensated Employees for such year, or (3) using the "bottom-up" method whereby such contribution shall be allocated to the Qualified Matching Contribution Account of the NonHighly Compensated Participant having the lowest 414(s) Compensation, until the applicable ACP test set forth in Section 12.7 is satisfied, or until such NonHighly Compensated Participant has received the lesser of the maximum "annual addition" pursuant to Section 4.4 or the maximum that may be taken into account in the ACP test pursuant to Section 12.4(g) (Targeted Contributions), continuing this process until one of the tests set forth in Section 12.7 is satisfied. ascensus_provision_text: Means Matching Contributions made pursuant to the Safe Harbor CODA formula described in Adoption Agreement Section Three, in an amount equal to (i) 100 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that do not exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation for the Plan Year, plus (ii) 50 percent of the amount of the Employee's Elective Deferrals that exceed three-percent of the Employee's Compensation but that do not exceed five-percent of the Employee's Compensation, if applicable. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: If a distribution is made to a Participant who has not severed employment and who is not fully Vested in the Participant's Account, and the Participant may increase the Vested percentage in such Account, then at any relevant time the Participant's Vested portion of the Account will be equal to an amount (X) determined by the formula: X = P (AB plus D). For purposes of applying the formula: P is the Vested percentage at the relevant time, AB is the Account balance at the relevant time, D is the amount of distribution, and the relevant time is the time at which, under the Plan, the Vested percentage in the Account cannot increase. ascensus_provision_text: Means nonforfeitable, that is, an unconditional and legally enforceable claim against the Plan that is obtained by a Participant or the Participant's Beneficiary to that part of an immediate or deferred benefit under the Plan that arises from a Participant's Years of Vesting Service. relius_provision_text: Notwithstanding any provision in the Plan to the contrary, distributions upon the death of a Participant shall comply with the requirements of Section 6.8. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: Amount of Required Minimum Distribution for Each Distribution Calendar Year
ascensus_provision_text:
1. Amount of Required Minimum Distribution for Each Distribution Calendar Year – During the Participant’s lifetime, the minimum amount that will be distributed for each Distribution Calendar Year is the lesser of:
  a. the quotient obtained by dividing the Participant’s Benefit by the distribution period in the Uniform Lifetime Table set forth in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-9, Q&A 2, using the Participant’s age as of the Participant’s birthday in the Distribution Calendar Year; or
  b. if the Participant’s sole Designated Beneficiary for the Distribution Calendar Year is the Participant’s Spouse, the quotient obtained by dividing the Participant’s Benefit by the number in the Joint and Last Survivor Table set forth in Treasury Regulation section 1.401(a)(9)-9, Q&A 3, using the Participant’s and Spouse’s attained ages as of the Participant’s and Spouse’s birthdays in the Distribution Calendar Year.

Additionally, the minimum amount that will be distributed for each Distribution Calendar Year will also include any payments from a Qualifying Longevity Annuity Contract where payments have commenced. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

The Relius provision emphasizes compliance with a specific section regarding distributions upon death, while the Ascensus provision details the calculation of RMDs during a participant's lifetime. The available options in the adoption agreements also differ significantly, indicating that they do not govern the same plan feature in a directly comparable manner.
variance_notes: Relius focuses on death-related distributions and compliance with a specific section, while Ascensus provides a formula for calculating RMDs during the participant's lifetime. The related adoption agreement elections also differ in scope and focus.

relius_provision_text: Coordination with ADP test. Notwithstanding the preceding, a Participant's Excess Deferrals shall be reduced, but not below zero, by any distribution and/or recharacterization of Excess Deferrals pursuant to Section 12.5(b) for the Plan Year beginning with or within the taxable year of the Participant. ascensus_provision_text: If the Employer elects to take Qualified Matching Contributions into account as Elective Deferrals for purposes of the ADP test, then (subject to such other requirements as may be prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury) the Employer may elect, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner, to either include all Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test or to include only the amount of such Qualified Matching Contributions that are needed to meet the ADP test. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (h) Discretionary safe harbor nonelective contribution. If the Employer has elected in the Adoption Agreement to either use the '401(k) safe harbor provisions' of this Section or to utilize the 'maybe' election with respect to the nonelective 'ADP test safe harbor contributions' for a Plan Year after the Plan Year has commenced in accordance with the provisions of this Subsection. In order to utilize this Section for the Plan Year, the Employer must provide a notice in accordance with Section 12.8(c)(4) above, except that the notice must provide that the Employer may provide the nonelective 'ADP test safe harbor contribution' and that a supplemental notice will be provided at least thirty (30) days prior to the last day of the Plan Year in order for the provisions of this Section to apply. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text:
ADP Test Safe Harbor Contributions – Unless such contributions are otherwise limited in the Adoption Agreement, the Employer will make the ADP Test Safe Harbor Contributions, if any, indicated in the Adoption Agreement on behalf of each Eligible Employee for the Plan Year.

The Employer may make ADP Test Safe Harbor Contributions at the same time as it contributes Elective Deferrals or at any other time as permitted by law and regulation.

If the Employer makes ADP Test Safe Harbor Contributions to the Plan based on Compensation earned and Elective Deferrals made during a portion of the Matching Contribution Computation Period (e.g., on a payroll basis when the Matching Contribution Computation Period is Plan Year), the Employer shall re-calculate ("true-up") the ADP Safe Harbor Contributions made to the Plan for that Plan Year. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

variance_notes: The Relius provision specifically mentions a discretionary safe harbor nonelective contribution and includes a notice requirement, while the Ascensus provision focuses on ADP test safe harbor contributions without explicitly mentioning discretionary contributions or notice requirements. relius_provision_text: An "in-Plan Roth rollover contribution" (IRR) means a rollover contribution to the Plan that consists of a distribution from a Participant's Plan account, other than a designated Roth account, that the Participant rolls over to the Participant's designated In-Plan Roth Rollover Contribution Account in the Plan, in accordance with Code §402(c)(4). ascensus_provision_text: Means any contribution made either as a Direct In-Plan Roth Rollover or an Indirect In-Plan Roth Rollover. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: the Administrator clearly informs the Participant that the Participant has a right to a period of thirty (30) days after receiving the notice to consider whether to waive the Joint and Survivor Annuity and to elect (with spousal consent) a form of distribution other than a Joint and Survivor Annuity; [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: FORM OF DISTRIBUTION TO A PARTICIPANT
ascensus_provision_text: Unless otherwise specified in the Adoption Agreement, if the value of the Vested portion of a Participant's Individual Account exceeds $1,000 and the Participant has properly waived the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity (if applicable), as described in Plan Section 5.10, the Participant may request (either in writing or in any other form permitted under rules promulgated by the IRS and DOL) that the Vested portion of their Individual Account be paid to them in one or more of the following forms of payment: 1) in a lump sum, 2) in a non-recurring partial payment, 3) in installment payments (a series of regularly scheduled recurring partial payments), or 4) applied to the purchase of an annuity contract. Notwithstanding the preceding, Qualifying Longevity Annuity Contracts may be distributed in any manner allowed under the Code or Treasury Regulations. In addition, non-recurring partial payments may be made from the Plan either before Termination of Employment or to satisfy the requirements of Code section 401(a)(9). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: For purposes of this Section, any amount paid to a child of the Participant will be treated as if it had been paid to the surviving Spouse if the amount becomes payable to the surviving Spouse when the child reaches the age of majority. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means any Spouse, former Spouse, child, or other dependent of a Participant who is recognized by a Domestic Relations Order as having a right to receive all, or a portion of, the benefits payable under the Plan with respect to such Participant. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_title: Suspension due to hardship or deemed severance.
relius_provision_text: Suspension due to hardship or deemed severance. In the event a Participant has received a hardship distribution pursuant to Regulation §1.401(k)-1(d)(3) from any other plan maintained by the Employer or from the Participant's Elective Deferral Account pursuant to Section 12.10, or has received a distribution on account of deemed severance on account of qualified military service from this Plan or any other plan maintained by the Employer, then such Participant shall not be permitted to elect to have Elective Deferrals contributed to the Plan in accordance with the rules set forth herein for such distributions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_title: Hardship Withdrawals of Matching Contributions and Employer Profit Sharing Contributions
ascensus_provision_text:
Hardship Withdrawals of Matching Contributions and Employer Profit Sharing Contributions — Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, if this is a profit sharing plan, then notwithstanding Plan Section 5.01(C)(1), an Employee may elect to receive a hardship distribution of all or part of the Vested portion of their Individual Account attributable to Employer Contributions other than those described in Plan Section 5.01(a)(2), subject to the requirements of Plan Section 5.10.

For purposes of this Plan Section 5.01(C)(2)(a), hardship is defined as an immediate and heavy financial need of the Employee where such Employee lacks other available resources. Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement, financial needs considered immediate and heavy include, but are not limited to: 1) expenses incurred or necessary for medical care, described in Code section 213(d), of the Employee, the Employee's Spouse, dependents, or, if elected, the Employee's Primary Beneficiary, 2) the purchase (excluding mortgage payments) of a principal residence for the Employee, 3) payment of tuition and related educational fees for the next 12 months of post-secondary education for the Employee, the Employee's Spouse, children, dependents, or, if elected, the Employee's Primary Beneficiary, 4) payment to prevent the eviction of the Employee from, or a foreclosure on the mortgage of, the Employee's principal residence, 5) funeral or burial expenses for the Employee's deceased parent, Spouse, child, dependent, or, if elected, the Employee's Primary Beneficiary, and 6) payment to repair damage to the Employee's principal residence that would qualify for a casualty loss deduction under Code section 165 (determined without regard to whether the loss exceeds ten-percent of adjusted gross income).

A distribution will be considered necessary to satisfy an immediate and heavy financial need of the Employee only if i. the Employee has obtained all distributions, other than hardship distributions, and all nontaxable loans available under all plans maintained by the Employer; and ii. the distribution is not in excess of the amount of an immediate and heavy financial need (including amounts necessary to pay any federal, state, or local income taxes or penalties reasonably anticipated to result from the distribution). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
reasoning: Both provisions address hardship distributions related to matching contributions, but they differ in their specific requirements and definitions of hardship. The Relius provision focuses on suspensions of contributions due to hardship or deemed severance, while the Ascensus provision outlines the conditions under which hardship withdrawals can be made from vested portions of accounts. The options available in the adoption agreements also differ in focus and structure.
variance_notes: Relius emphasizes suspension of contributions after a hardship distribution, while Ascensus allows for hardship withdrawals from vested contributions. The definitions of hardship and the specific financial needs considered differ between the two provisions.

relius_title: Elimination of safe harbor contributions or matching contributions.
relius_provision_text: (i) Elimination of safe harbor contributions or matching contributions. The Employer may amend the Plan during a Plan Year to reduce or eliminate 'ADP test safe harbor contributions' or matching contributions for such Plan Year subject to the following provisions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means any Basic Matching Contributions, Enhanced Matching Contributions, and Safe Harbor Nonelective Contributions under either the Safe Harbor CODA provisions or the QACA provisions. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (i)(1) An amendment may be made during a Plan Year to reduce or eliminate prospectively any or all 'ADP test safe harbor contributions' provided a supplemental notice is given to all 'eligible Participants' explaining the consequences and effective date of the amendment, and that such 'eligible Participants' have a reasonable opportunity (including a reasonable period) to change their Elective Deferral (and if applicable, their Voluntary Employee Contribution) elections. An amendment reducing or eliminating an 'ADP test safe harbor contribution' must be effective no earlier than the later of: (i) thirty (30) days after 'eligible Participants' are given the supplemental notice or (ii) the date the amendment is adopted. If the Employer amends the Plan to reduce or eliminate the 'ADP test safe harbor contributions,' then except as provided in Code §§401(k) and 401(m) and the Regulations thereunder, the Plan is subject to the ADP test set forth in Section 12.4 and the ACP test set forth in Section 12.6 for the entire Plan Year using current year testing and the Employer must also satisfy the provisions of this Section 12.8 until the amendment becomes effective. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

[Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (i) General. For purposes of this Article, the term "qualified optional survivor annuity" means an annuity: (A) For the life of the Participant with a survivor annuity for the life of the Participant's Spouse which is equal to the "applicable percentage" of the amount of the annuity which is payable during the joint lives of the Participant and the Participant's Spouse, and (B) Which is the actuarial equivalent of a single annuity for the life of the Participant. Such term also includes any annuity in a form having the effect of an annuity described in the preceding sentence. (ii) Applicable percentage. For purposes of this Subsection, the "applicable percentage" is based on the survivor annuity percentage (i.e., the percentage which the survivor annuity under the Plan's qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity bears to the annuity payable during the joint lives of the Participant and the Participant's Spouse). If the survivor annuity percentage is less than seventy-five percent (75%), then the "applicable percentage" is seventy-five percent (75%); otherwise, the "applicable percentage" is fifty percent (50%). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: Means an annuity 1) for the life of the Participant with a survivor annuity for the life of the Spouse that is equal to the "applicable percentage" of the amount of the annuity that is payable during the joint lives of the Participant and the Spouse, and 2) that is the actuarial equivalent of a single annuity for the life of the Participant. If the survivor annuity provided by the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity is less than 75 percent of the annuity payable during the joint lives of the Participant and the Spouse, the applicable percentage is 75 percent. If the survivor annuity provided by the Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity is greater than or equal to 75 percent, the applicable percentage is 50 percent. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_title: Adjustment due to anticipated failure of ADP test.
relius_provision_text: If during a Plan Year, it is projected that the aggregate amount of Elective Deferrals to be allocated to all Highly Compensated Participants under this Plan would cause the Plan to fail the tests set forth in Section 12.4, then the Administrator may automatically reduce the Elective Deferrals of affected Highly Compensated Participants, beginning with the Highly Compensated Participant who has the highest actual deferral ratio until it is anticipated the Plan will pass the tests or until the actual deferral ratio equals the actual deferral ratio of the Highly Compensated Participant having the next highest actual deferral ratio. This process may continue until it is anticipated that the Plan will satisfy one of the tests set forth in Section 12.4. Alternatively, the Employer may specify a maximum percentage of Compensation that may be deferred by Highly Compensated Participants (and any such limitation shall be a Plan-imposed limit for purposes of determining Catch-up Contributions). [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]
ascensus_provision_text: In the event that the Plan Administrator determines that it is not likely that the ADP test will be satisfied for a particular Plan Year unless certain steps are taken before the end of such Plan Year, the Plan Administrator may require Contributing Participants who are Highly Compensated Employees to reduce or cease making Elective Deferrals under this paragraph, the reduction or cessation will begin with the Highly Compensated Employee with either the largest amount of Elective Deferrals or the highest Contribution Percentage for the Plan Year (on the date on which it is determined that the ADP test will not likely be satisfied), as elected by the Plan Administrator. All remaining Highly Compensated Employees will be permitted to enroll again as Contributing Participants in accordance with the terms of the Plan. [Linked to 5 AA election(s)]

relius_provision_text: (1) Automatic contribution arrangements. ascensus_provision_text: ACA provisions – Each Employee who satisfies the eligibility requirements specified in the Adoption Agreement for Elective Deferrals will be given a reasonable opportunity to enroll as a Contributing Participant. Unless otherwise elected in the Adoption Agreement or as otherwise indicated in rules promulgated by the IRS, Elective Deferrals made to the Plan pursuant to the ACA provisions will be subject to any other Plan rules otherwise applicable to Elective Deferrals.
